skill.name: expo-cicd-workflows
skill.description: Helps developers create, edit, and validate Expo EAS workflow YAML files using the current official schema and workflow documentation.
skill.summary: Create and validate Expo EAS CI/CD workflow YAML files with schema-backed guidance.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the apparent weak-crypto and shell-backtick detections are false positives after review. The confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated: helper scripts fetch official Expo workflow resources, read workflow files, write a local cache, and the skill instructs agents to run Node-based validation commands.

medium_findings.title: Network Fetching of Workflow Reference Data
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts fetch the current Expo workflow schema and documentation from Expo-controlled URLs. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it introduces network dependency and remote-content trust considerations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call and fixed schema URL are directly visible, and the skill explicitly requires fetching these resources. The URLs are official Expo resources, so this is a legitimate elevated risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Run Node Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash execution for Node commands and instructs agents to run helper scripts and install script dependencies. This is expected for workflow validation, but it expands execution risk compared with documentation-only skills.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and command examples are explicit. The commands target local helper scripts, so the risk is from permitted execution scope rather than evidence of command injection.

low_findings.title: Local Cache and Workflow File Access
low_findings.description: The fetch helper creates a local cache and writes fetched data, while the validator reads user-specified workflow files. This matches the documented purpose and does not show evidence of unauthorized file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem reads and writes are directly present. The paths are either the cache directory or user-provided workflow files for validation, making the observed behavior expected and limited.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick findings are JavaScript template literals, Markdown code fences, inline code, or console output strings. No Ruby backtick execution or shell command interpolation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed contexts show formatting or JavaScript strings, not shell execution syntax. No dynamic child_process, eval, or Ruby-style backtick execution was present in these locations.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The only cryptographic API usage hashes a URL with SHA-256 to create a cache filename prefix. SHA-256 is not weak in this context and is not used for passwords, signatures, or encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly uses createHash with sha256 only for deterministic cache naming. There is no security-sensitive cryptographic decision or weak algorithm evidence at this location.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: No text was found that claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or attempts to override the security review process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed skill text contains task instructions for Expo workflow assistance, not evaluator-directed override language. This assessment is limited to the three audited files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Fetch Call
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch helper performs outbound HTTP requests to retrieve schema and documentation resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call is direct and is used by validation logic. It targets documented Expo resources rather than an unknown endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read and Cache Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts read workflow files and write cached network responses under the scripts cache directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem calls are explicit and support the validator workflow. No evidence of broad scanning or secret harvesting was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the agent to run Node helper commands for fetching and validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented directly in the skill. They are bounded to Node helper scripts, but they still require agent command execution.

content.value_statement: Expo teams need workflow files that match current EAS syntax and schema rules. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create, edit, and validate EAS workflow YAML with official references.
actual_capabilities: Fetches the current official EAS workflow schema before generating workflow guidance.
Creates and edits workflow YAML files under .eas/workflows for Expo projects.
Validates workflow files against the official schema using a bundled Node validator.
Explains supported triggers, jobs, runner options, expressions, and workflow structure.
Checks job references, dependencies, expressions, and required fields for common mistakes.
limitations: Requires network access to fetch the latest Expo schema and documentation.
Does not deploy apps or run EAS builds by itself.
Validation quality depends on the availability and accuracy of the official schema endpoint.
May require installing Node dependencies inside the skill scripts directory.

use_cases.title: Create a Release Workflow
use_cases.description: Generate an EAS workflow that builds and submits an Expo app for production release.

use_cases.title: Debug Workflow Validation Errors
use_cases.description: Review an existing workflow file and fix schema, trigger, dependency, or expression issues.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Automation
use_cases.description: Create consistent workflow patterns for pull requests, previews, internal builds, and production deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new workflow file
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Expo EAS workflow for my app that runs on pull requests and builds a preview version. Use the current schema.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Existing YAML
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of a workflow file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review .eas/workflows/preview.yml, validate it against the current EAS schema, and explain the fixes needed.

prompt_templates.title: Add Release Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my EAS workflow to build iOS and Android, submit to stores, and keep job dependencies clear.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Environment Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design EAS workflows for preview, staging, and production releases with manual inputs, concurrency controls, and reusable defaults.

output_examples.input: Create a workflow for preview builds on pull requests.
output_examples.output: A schema-aligned workflow plan with pull request triggers, preview build jobs, required parameters, and validation notes.

output_examples.input: Why does my workflow fail validation?
output_examples.output: A concise error review that identifies invalid fields, missing required values, broken dependencies, and recommended corrections.

output_examples.input: List available EAS workflow job types.
output_examples.output: A current summary derived from the fetched Expo schema, with job purposes and important parameters.

best_practices: Fetch the current Expo workflow schema before creating or changing workflow files.
Validate every edited workflow before considering the CI/CD change complete.
Keep job names, dependencies, and expressions simple enough for teammates to review.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on memorized EAS job names or outdated examples.
Do not skip validation after editing workflow YAML.
Do not hardcode secrets directly in workflow files.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create, edit, explain, and validate Expo EAS workflow YAML files.

faq.question: Does it use the latest EAS workflow syntax?
faq.answer: It is designed to fetch the current official schema and documentation before giving schema-specific guidance.

faq.question: Can it run builds or submit apps?
faq.answer: No. It helps define workflow files, but EAS still runs the actual build and submit jobs.

faq.question: Why does it need network access?
faq.answer: It fetches the official Expo workflow schema and documentation so generated workflows use current options.

faq.question: Why does it include scripts?
faq.answer: The scripts fetch reference data with caching and validate workflow YAML against the official schema.

faq.question: Is this suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
